verdict_reasoning: BAX is canonically cytosolic/mitochondrial-outer-membrane — it resides as a soluble monomer in the cytoplasm under healthy conditions and inserts into the mitochondrial outer membrane upon apoptotic activation. However, during apoptosis and immunogenic cell death, BAX translocates to and permeabilizes the mitochondrial outer membrane, and there is documented evidence of BAX exposure on the plasma membrane outer leaflet of apoptotic/dying cells. Multiple studies report surface-accessible BAX on apoptotic tumor cells detectable by antibody staining of intact cells, qualifying it as a cell-state-induced ecto-pool. This places BAX firmly in the contextual/cell_state_induced bucket — not stably surface-displayed at baseline, but documented on the outer leaflet during apoptotic/ICD states relevant to therapeutic targeting.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and consistency of outer-leaflet BAX exposure on intact apoptotic cells vs. secondary necrosis / membrane disruption artefact is not fully resolved; PM display may reflect membrane breach rather than regulated translocation.
